The rest of the building was evacuated, and by the time firefighters got the blaze under control, the woman inside apartment 8 had stopped responding.
As officers eventually made entry, they found her half-burnt body in the bedroom.
Initial investigation would reveal that this wasn't just a fire, but the cover-up for a brutal crime.
Multiple witnesses had seen an unidentified man in the victim's apartment window as the fire first started.

Witnesses believed the victim was with her boyfriend that same day, and they were heard arguing at the apartment the night before.
Shana was a breast cancer survivor, who was continuing recovery from her April 2020 diagnosis, chemotherapy, and double mastectomy.
The boyfriend, Melvin Bilbro Jr., then 41, had gone to Shana Daniels' apartment 15 minutes before the officers arrived.
At the time, Bilbro was a four-time convicted felon on supervised release after serving prison time for failing to register as a predatory offender.
He was released from prison just five months before the fire.

His past convictions in Minnesota included attempted second-degree murder of his then girlfriend, and for second-degree criminal sexual conduct involving her 12-year-old daughter in 2008, and for third-degree assault involving another girlfriend in 2019.
Police obtained a search warrant for both Shana and her boyfriend's apartment and found key evidence, a blood stained pack of Newport cigarettes.
Blood on walls.
Scissors and a broken knife covered in blood.
Meanwhile, in Bilbro's apartment, more evidence was found.
One of the clearest pieces of evidence against Bilbro was a dog covered in soot and smelling of smoke.
The dog found in his apartment belonged to Shana.
It became clear, this wasn't just a tragic fire.
It was the scene of a murder.
The autopsy reports revealed that the cause of Shana's death was not an accidental fire, but homicide.
She had multiple sharp force injuries to her head and neck with a perforation to her left eye.
Her nasal bone was fractured.
Police officers spoke to a witness who called Bilbro and told him of the fire.
Bilbro said that he was with Shana when her ex-boyfriend showed up, so he left.
When his apartment was searched, he was nowhere to be found.

Shana grew up in North Minneapolis and was remembered by friends and family members, not only for her courageous battle with breast cancer, but for her outgoing, positive energy, her love of animals, and for her fearless spirit.
Family wrote that quote, Although her life was taken so abruptly, we are so grateful God blessed us with her beautiful presence.
In January 2024, Bilbro waived his right to a jury trial and instead opted to have the judge be the sole decider of his guilt.
Ramsey County Judge David Brown reviewed all the submitted evidence and arguments from both sides.

He wrote in an 84-page filing that a pattern of continued domestic abuse was proven by prosecutors, and they had proven beyond a reasonable doubt that Bilbro was the one who killed Shana, set the fire at the apartment, and fled the scene.
On February 2nd, 2024, Judge Brown found Bilbro guilty of first-degree murder, second-degree murder, and first-degree arson.
He was sentenced to life in prison and remains in custody in Stillwater without the possibility of parole.
